sentences.text: Yes.
I used to--
Just down the road and up the hill.
I recall that very distinctly.
And there was a camp, obviously surrounded by barbed wire.
And I remember the prisoners.
I remember the initials, POW.
Of course, they didn't mean anything to me.
I thought it was a French word of some kind.
[LAUGHS]
No, they wore-- the prisoners had on the-- their uniforms.
Whatever they wore had POW on it-- I recall that-- of course, guarded by the Americans.
And I would just go in there and watch them.
And--
Yes.
Well, they would come in the cafe.
And I was the--
Nice, yes, very nice.
And I was a little puzzled because there was an American soldier.
He was a black man.
And I had never seen a black person in my life.
So I was a little intrigued.
And he was very nice, I recall.
Yes, yes.
I, I thought it was funny because I thought he was made up.
No, it was-- that's just, just the color of the skin.
And I guess I had to accept that, you know.
[LAUGHS] But it was-- it was just very intriguing to a little child, having never seen a black man.
There was also, I recall, a French man.
And he was in the army.
And I said, how could you be French and be in the American Army?
Puzzling also-- I remember that.
And they would come in and give me all kinds of chocolates.
I don't think I recall having had chocolates as a child.
And I remember that very much.
Yes, very much.
[LAUGHS] They would bring me all kinds of treats.
Yeah, they were very nice.
Yes, oh yes.
They were-- it was-- almost felt like it was no longer a cafe, more like a, a big family, yeah.
I'm sure they were-- yeah, mostly probably officers would come in and just sit and talk and inquire, you know, just asked us questions and--
Oh yes.
[SIGHS] More relaxed.
Not only that, Evelyn stopped going out at night.
[LAUGHS] She didn't have to do that anymore.
And it was just totally different.
I also stopped going to catechism.
Yes, oh yes, definitely.
But I didn't have to go to church anymore.
And I had to-- I didn't have to go to catechism.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember being told that.
I wasn't very happy in the first place, anyway.
And I really was always a very good student.
But I never learned anything anyway.
It went in one ear and out the other.
Yes, because the war was over.
And I didn't have to.
Don't forget, they were Protestant.
So there was no reason for them to make me go to a Catholic church.
And pretend to be not Jewish.
Right, yes.
Yes.
So that was a relief.
And there was definitely a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] different atmosphere.
You felt free.
The bombing stopped.
The leaflets stopped falling from the sky.
And the Germans were gone.
Other than-- and I think that's why I was totally delighted to see them behind the fences.
It was their turn.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yeah, it became a, a more relaxed time.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] it was the end.
I remember cheering in the streets, yeah.
No, I didn't feel anything.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't think there was any time in my life that I was immersed in Judaism per se.
Only why was I different?
I couldn't understand it.
Yes.
Yes, oh yes, from that time when the German had asked my mother, you know, why?
[CRYING] And I guess, at that time, I must have asked my mother, why do I have to wear a star?
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she had to give me, you know, the reason, that we were Jewish and we're held apart.
Yes.
Not so much assimilated-- no, I don't think it's-- you can call it assimilated.
I think you call it-- what would you say that they were free of practicing religion?
They were not-- they did not consider themselves assimilated.
I think, if anything, the, the Yiddishkeit was there at all times.
There was-- Yiddish was my first language.
I had to learn French, even though I was French born.
And no, it was just a total escape from structured religion.
But in no way, I don't think they were assimilated.
I think their friends, everybody, was Jewish.
There was no assimilation then, no.
But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I just couldn't understand why we would be ostracized.
And how do you explain to a child?
So I had no understanding of that.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But I knew my identity.
[CRYING] I did, yeah.
So you went from this-- the safer existence with the Cailacs--
Yes.
Yes.
Right.
Withdrawn, very shy, very reserved-- I think that's what I-- you know, I became that-- and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] having tantrums, later on.
No.
Yes, uh-huh, crying fits.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [CRYING] not knowing where I stood-- [CRYING] [SOBBING] feeling safe in one place and being taken away.
And being sent here and sent there.
So I didn't want it-- I don't-- I didn't want it to happen.
[CRYING] [SOBBING] So I was unhappy.
To my uncle, yes.
My aunt, no, no.
Yes.
To another apartment in Paris.
Oh, yes.
That's 7, Cite Voltaire.
It was a different neighborhood.
It wasn't a very big apartment.
Yes, yes, much smaller.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had to sleep in the living room.
So-- but I went to school.
Yes, then there was a school in Paris, yes.
[SIGHS] It's called Cite Voltaire Primary Grade.
And I would go.
After that, I was sent [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to my cousin's wedding in London-- my cousin Stella.
And--
Yes.
Well, it was her stepmother.
But it was her father.
And I remember I went in the wintertime.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That was, I guess at that point, 1947.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I stayed just a week or so for the wedding.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had to go back to Paris.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And from then on, I was sent every year to London to spend my summer vacations, first with my uncle until he died, and then with my cousin Stella afterwards, until I was 18.
Yes.
Yes, yes.
Yes, [CRYING] very much, yes.
Oh, they were all Jewish children.
They were all the children that had been found or-- I believe it was run by ORT.
Yes, definitely-- was definitely all Jewish children who were all orphans.
No.
No, no, [CRYING] because I guess [SOBBING] we all had our little stories, and-- no.
Not really.
Just-- you know, I think we were all a bit traumatized.
[CRYING] There were a lot of-- a, you know, big span of-- you know, from younger than me-- or just about my age-- to teenagers, until homes were found for all of us, yeah.
I think my aunt had been traumatized by the war and, I think, had lost a, a child in infancy, and I don't think was very accepting of me.
[CRYING] So it was a little hard.
[SOBBING] My uncle was very kind.
But it was hard for me, very hard.
I couldn't-- always felt somewhat of a stranger in their home.
I didn't go.
I went back to Andresy only after I'd married, to show my husband.
I didn't go back.
I've never gone back--
--to the Ardennes.
Andresy was the orphanage.
I never went back to the Ardennes at all.
Yes.
[SIGHS] Not really.
I don't think our movements were very free when we were in Paris.
No, I did not really recall anything.
I actually recall being on vacation at the beach with my parents.
But not--
No, yeah, Les Sables de-- Les Sables d'Olonne.
It's on the west coast, obviously, of France, on the Mediterranean-- on the Atlantic side, yes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But now I have the feeling that probably our movements in Paris were pretty restricted, yeah.
Oh, yes, the furnishings-- the furniture-- there was still furniture.
I couldn't say whether things had been stolen, which maybe they were.
But basically, the apartment was in-- never lived in by anyone from the time my parents had left it.
So I guess the authorities left it intact.
I believe so.
But my aunt and uncle took everything because they were-- they didn't have anything.
So it gave them a chance to give them a start.
Because they came out of Italy with absolutely nothing.
Resentful.
It was mine.
No, no.
No.
[CRYING] Nobody cared to talk about them, so no.
[CRYING] Once in awhile I would ask my uncle.
And he would just give me a brief answer.
[SOBBING] So no.
[CRYING] And my aunt didn't.
So actually it was more my cousin, Stella, who would tell me about my father.
Because she had lost her mother before the war.
She was quite a-- she was 15 years older than me.
So he would take her to France as a little girl on vacation.
And she was happy to do the same for me, [CRYING] yeah.
And so she had real memories of your father?
Yes, absolutely.
Yeah, yes.
And of course, her father.
No, no, I believe after they married, he stopped sending for her, [LAUGHS] yeah, yeah.
Yeah, eventually I did, yeah.
Yes, I went to school, primary grade first.
And then I went on to high school, high school combination college.
And I was skipped two years.
I skipped two grades.
So I went on at 17-- 16-- I went to London and went to college for a year and a half there, for two years.
And I stayed with my cousin Stella at that point.
Yes.
I went to business.
I studied business.
And at 18-- 17 and 1/2, 18-- I went back to Paris, and went back to my aunt and uncle, and started to go to work.
I went into secretarial-- secretary.
And because I was bilingual, French and English, I got a bilingual position immediately.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] went to-- I used to belong to a choir run by a youth organization from a, a-- Jewish.
And they had organized what they called a Zimriyah for-- to attract young people in Israel.
And I had to pay a minimal fee with my whole group to go to Israel.
So we were treated in 1955 to a very nice trip hosted by the Israeli government to try and attract settlers and had a lovely time.
Yes.
Yes, yes.
No, no.
I think I had enough disruptions in my life.
And--
Oh yeah, oh yeah.
Well, they sort of cheated.
We had students from the opera join the choir.
[LAUGHS] And they were not Jewish.
And they stayed and converted.
So I think it served a purpose.
Yes, they were totally overwhelmed by what they-- I mean it was just incredible seeing little trees like this growing in the desert.
And I finally met my Uncle Jakob that had been in Palestine all along.
It was my father's oldest brother.
Just-- I met him briefly.
He'd didn't have any children.
So I didn't have any cousins to meet.
And when I came back, I met my husband.
How did I meet my husband?
It was Rosh Hashanah.
And two friends of mine were walking around.
And we went-- we had just went to see if the Jewish Center where we belonged to as singers was open.
It wasn't.
But the director lived upstairs.
And we rang the bell.
Anyway, he said, well, you know, oh how nice.
I have two Americans here.
Would you like to [LAUGHS] meet them?
One of them was my husband.
[LAUGHS] So we met on Rosh Hashanah.
And our first date was on Yom Kippur.
[LAUGHS] Now were you and your aunt and uncle at this point observant--
No.
No.
Oh, yes.
Like I said, yes--
I-- over the years, I was taught Yiddish, how to write.
I went and got some--
Yes, I got Yiddish education, more than towards the Hebrew.
And, like I said, my uncle was from the same school as my father.
My, my uncle was an ordained rabbi.
And it was a total escape from religion, which came back to him as he grew older.
But he would--
My Uncle Gabriel in Paris.
Yes, who I was living with-- had the same feelings.
In Poland.
Oh yes, yes.
Yes, he had, yeah.
But while I was in England with my cousin Stella and my-- my Uncle Leon was the same thing in London, not religious.
But his daughter, Stella, really became religious.
And I got a good basis there from the summers and going to temple, and, you know, having Shabbos, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] seeing Jewish religion and going to-- being observant.
And usually I was there for the holidays.
I'd go to temple.
I was forced to fast.
But it's fine.
[LAUGHS] When I turned 13 they said, you had to fast.
I said, OK.
And so I got my Jewish education, basically, more from my cousin in London than from any of my two uncles.
Yes, oh yes.
Yes.
Yes, definitely, yes.
That was-- I, I mean, from 11 on-- I-- no, 12 on-- I was going to England by myself from Paris.
I'd be on the train in Paris and get off the train, get on the boat, cross the channel, get back on the train to go to London.
And they were waiting for me at the station, yes.
And same back in-- same in reverse, yes.
Yeah.
So I--
His name is Philip Fiol.
And like I said, we met on Rosh Hashanah.
And it was a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] quick courtship, I guess, in today's--
He was in the army.
He had been stationed in Germany and moved to France.
And because it was Rosh Hashanah, he was looking for a Jewish environment.
He felt, I guess, homesick.
He and his friend, they were both Jewish.
I think they had given them in their little booklets from the army where to find Jewish houses of worship and stuff.
So we met on Rosh Hashanah.
Yes.
That was 1955, yeah.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I went out with his friend.
[LAUGHS] He went out with my girlfriend.
And I believe he asked his-- my girlfriend, are you-- or his friend, are you interested in her?
He said, no, not really.
He says, can I go with her?
[LAUGHS] So this is how we started our courtship.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] oh yeah, my cousin in England, Stella, had-- was on the verge of convincing my aunt and my Uncle Gabriel from Paris to move to England.
Because she really wanted me there, too.
And she felt it was a way to move all of us to England.
And we were in the process of doing that.
But, of course, when I told them that I had met a young man, I called them up and said, I'm going to be marrying an American.
They said, don't do anything.
We'll be here next weekend.
[LAUGHS] They came to Paris, tried to stop it.
Yes, they did.
I believe they offered him money.
Well, that's all they had to do.
[LAUGHS] And we were married the next October.
I guess they realized they couldn't do anything.
They actually even offered the two of us to move to England, which we promptly refused, you know--
Possibly, but the fact that they hadn't done a great impression in the beginning with him wasn't too convincing at the time.
Yes, I was torn.
But I really went along with him because I would-- probably had-- you know, I was just as insulted at the time.
It was--
Happy.
I was happy.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes, in control of my life, first time, [CRYING] where I could dictate how I want to live my life, yeah.
So--
Yes, yeah.
And so in '57-- six months, I guess, later, until all the paperwork for the Army were completed for me-- we came to New York.
Interesting experience.
[LAUGHS]
No, because it was the Hungarian Revolution.
And all the refugees were being flown to the United States.
So we had to come by boat.
And I crossed the ocean [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] being seasick for 14 days.
You want to lose weight?
That's the way to do it.
And--
Yes, I do.
Yes, it was in April 7, 1957, Fort Hamilton, Brooklyn, New York.
[LAUGHS] I think-- my first step into New York, and I'm in-- you know, in the United States-- and I land in Brooklyn.
